Kozłówka Palace, 35 km from Lublin, houses two unusual collections under one roof: Count Zamoyski's apartments, preserved in their late-19th-century state, and a museum of Soviet socialist realism—portraits of Stalin, busts of Lenin, propaganda posters—salvaged after 1989. The juxtaposition is dizzying.

activities.text: Exploring Lublin's underground network: 280 meters of medieval cellars connect 14 buildings beneath the Old Town. This fitted-out route tells the story of the city's merchant history in the 15th-17th centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Majdanek is the world's oldest concentration camp memorial, opened as early as autumn 1944 on 90 hectares. Barracks, gas chambers, crematorium and mausoleum are in their original state. Plan at least two hours. Free entry, audio guide available. This is not a 'tourist site': it's a place of remembrance.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Stand in awe before Malbork Castle, the largest castle in the world by surface area and a UNESCO World Heritage masterpiece of medieval brick Gothic architecture. This colossal fortress served as the headquarters of the Teutonic Knights who ruled vast territories of Northern Europe from these imposing walls. The castle complex includes the High Castle, Middle Castle and Lower Castle, each revealing layers of medieval political power and religious devotion.

Explore the castle's magnificent interior chambers, including the Grand Master's Palace with its stunning vaulted ceilings and the atmospheric cellars and passages that wind beneath the fortress. The castle museum displays medieval armor, weapons and artifacts that bring the Teutonic era vividly to life. Evening sound and light shows illuminate the castle walls, creating an unforgettable spectacle against the night sky.

activities.text: Walk the castle's extensive defensive walls and towers for panoramic views over the Nogat River and surrounding countryside

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Wilczki Falls plunges 22 meters into the so-called American canyon—25-meter vertical walls, thunderous echo—and it's the second-highest waterfall in the Polish Sudetes, after Kamieńczyk Falls. Before the 1997 flood, a 19th-century artificial sill raised it to 27 meters. The Wilczki Waterfall Nature Reserve has been protected since 1958.

The roar of water ricochets off the American canyon's walls: even in dry weather, spray soaks spectators on the lower walkways. In winter, the cascade freezes into spectacular icicle towers. Paid parking 200 m away, 10-minute walk up wooden stairs. Free access to the waterfall year-round.

activities.text: Take the red trail to the MB Śnieżna sanctuary on Igliczna (845 m)—1.5 hours from the village, beech forest and open views over the Śnieżnik massif. Międzygórze is the classic starting point for a hike to Śnieżnik. The return trail via Górka passes a fairy-tale sculpture garden, appreciated with children.

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone

visits.text: Międzyrzecz's medieval castle dominates the Obra river from the old town ramparts. Founded in the Middle Ages and remodeled during the Renaissance, it houses the Regional Museum with archaeological and local history collections. The tower offers unobstructed views over the valley. Variable opening hours - check in advance.

activities.text: For military history enthusiasts, the "dragon's teeth" route - anti-tank concrete obstacles scattered across agricultural fields between Pniewo and Boryszyn - can be covered by bike in 1 hour 30 minutes. Explanatory panels mark the route. Bike rental available in Międzyrzecz center.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: Stroll along Międzyzdroje's famous Promenade of Stars, where handprints of beloved Polish movie stars are embedded in the pavement like a Baltic Hollywood Walk of Fame. This elegant seaside resort boasts one of the most beautiful beaches on the Polish coast, with fine sand stretching beneath dramatic coastal cliffs. The historic pier extends far into the Baltic Sea, offering romantic sunset views and fresh sea breezes.

Explore the Baltic Miniature Park where meticulously crafted 1:25 scale models showcase the most important monuments of Baltic countries. Each miniature faithfully reproduces architectural details of famous landmarks, creating a fascinating geography and history lesson in one enchanting location. The park also features playgrounds, a miniature train ride and family-friendly amenities.

Journey back to the Middle Ages at the Slavs and Vikings Centre in Wolin, an archaeological open-air museum recreating life over a thousand years ago. Visitors can explore reconstructed huts, workshops and authentic equipment used by the Slavic and Viking peoples who once inhabited this region. Craft demonstrations, warrior combat shows and educational workshops bring ancient history dramatically to life.

Visit Wolin National Park to encounter European bison at the renowned Bison Show Farm, where these majestic animals can be observed up close along with deer and wild boars. The park features numerous hiking trails, scenic viewpoints and educational paths that wind through pristine coastal forests. The Natural History Museum presents the region's diverse fauna and flora through engaging dioramas and interactive exhibitions.

activities.text: Walk the educational trails of Wolin National Park to discover the unique ecosystems of this protected coastal wilderness

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
West Pomerania: Szczecin, Baltic Sea & Lakes

visits.text: Flat dikes, reeds stretching to the horizon, bird cries: the Milicz Ponds open another world. This string of water bodies spreads through the Barycz Valley Landscape Park, Poland's largest, and serves as refuge for countless species. The calm is absolute. Come at sunrise or sunset—that's when light and birds give their best.

activities.text: Park near a pond and follow the dikes on foot or by bike. You'll encounter ospreys, swans, and rare ducks threading through the reed beds. An observation tower lets you scan without disturbing. Bring binoculars and sturdy shoes—some paths turn muddy after rain.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: Lower Silesia and Greater Poland in 5 Days

visits.text: Rawer and wilder than its neighbor, Mirów Castle raises its Gothic ruins on a limestone spur. Also built in Casimir the Great's era, it remained long abandoned and is only partly restored by the same owner as Bobolice. The hill is open for a small entrance fee; the ruins themselves remain partly closed, but the silhouette is worth the look.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: From Wrocław to the Holy Cross Mountains in 5 Days

visits.text: Don't come here for summits: Wielkopolski National Park is a lowland preserve, Poland's seventh-oldest. Come instead for the light on Lake Góreckie and the ruins of Klaudyna Potocka's castle, romantically crumbling on its island in the middle of the lake. Pay the parking fee (5 PLN) and set aside at least two hours.

From the viewpoint on the western shore, Wyspa Zamkowa reflects perfectly in the waters of Lake Góreckie - a 19th-century neo-Gothic castle collapsing into elegant ruins. The red trail leading to the lookout follows roughly 2 km of wooded shoreline. Arrive early in the morning before weekend families from Poznań pour in.

activities.text: You'll follow the 'Szlak Kosynierów' (Scythemen's Trail) that traces Lake Góreckie from Osowa Góra to Puszczykowo - 14.5 km total for a full day, or 4 km round-trip for a shorter outing. Walking shoes recommended: parts of the trail edge can get muddy. Check parking lot hours before 6 PM.

days_plan.itin_title: Greater Poland: Birthplace of the Polish State in 9 Days

visits.text: If blood pressure concerns you, Nałęczów is Poland's only cardiological resort. The valley's microclimate, loess ravines and four mineral springs create natural therapeutic conditions. The spa park (Park Zdrojowy), founded in the eighteenth century, counts some sixty tree species.
The villa of Stefan Żeromski, Polish novelist (1864–1925), can be visited on the hill above the park. Żeromski lived and wrote here. Small literary museum, fin-de-siècle atmosphere. Modest entry.

activities.text: Taste the ferruginous water at the Pijalnia (pump room) in the park, in a greenhouse-shaped building with busts of benefactors. The taste is harsh, but it's the local ritual. Free.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Locals simply call this place "the skansen," as if no other existed nearby. The Muzeum Etnograficzne w Ochli spreads across 13 hectares of forest edge and brings together more than 100 rural buildings relocated from Lower Silesia, western Greater Poland and Lower Lusatia. The oldest building - a Potrzebowo house from 1675 - survived three centuries intact. Closed Mondays.

Picturesque and bucolic, but this village-museum hides a more bitter layer of history: it houses objects brought from the eastern Kresy by Poles expelled after 1945. The 18th-century wine tower from Budachow stands among replanted vines - one of the rare witnesses to pre-war local viticulture. House interiors are furnished as they were in the 19th century, without flashy staging.

activities.text: Taste the wine produced in the estate's own vineyards, planted on the museum grounds themselves. In summer season (June-August), the karczma (inn) offers regional Lubuskie specialties paired with house wine. Museum admission: around 25 PLN per adult. The skansen's island organizes group barbecues on request.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: Ogrodzieniec Castle is the largest ruin on the Eagles' Nests Trail, standing on the highest point of the Kraków-Częstochowa Jura. Built in the fourteenth century then transformed into a Renaissance residence, it was ravaged during the Swedish Deluge of 1655 and abandoned in 1810. The huge white boulders built into the walls create a striking scene. The site gets very busy: arrive early in high season.

activities.text: Climb onto the battlements and white boulders of Ogrodzieniec, which became the backdrop for shoots like The Witcher series. From May to September, chivalric brotherhoods stage reenactments, and the site hides a tower to climb and a torture chamber. Pack water in summer—the hilltop is bare, with no shade.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: From Wrocław to the Holy Cross Mountains in 5 Days

visits.text: Olsztyn Castle, built in the fourteenth century by Casimir the Great, opens the Eagles' Nests Trail, that chain of fortresses raised on limestone crags between Kraków and Częstochowa. Taken by the Swedes then besieged in the sixteenth century, only spectacular ruins remain, accessible for a small fee. Good shoes recommended—the ground is uneven.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: From Wrocław to the Holy Cross Mountains in 5 Days

visits.text: The Piast Tower, standing roughly 42 meters tall, is the last remnant of Opole's Gothic castle, demolished in the twentieth century. You can climb to the top for a view over the city and the Oder, then browse a small exhibition inside. Check the opening hours for the ascent—they vary with the season.

It looks like miniature Florence: Opole's town hall openly borrows from the Palazzo Vecchio. It presides over the Rynek, a square ringed with colorful houses in the heart of a city laced with canals—hence the nickname Polish Venice. Walk the square's perimeter; you can see it all in half an hour.

activities.text: Push on to the Millennium Amphitheatre, home to the National Festival of Polish Song, held there every year since 1963. The adjacent Museum of Polish Song traces the history of this event followed across the country. Outside festival season, the site is quiet—a good moment to visit the museum without crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: From Wrocław to the Holy Cross Mountains in 5 Days

places.name: Ostrow Lednicki

visits.text: The ferry access changes everything. Ostrów Lednicki is an island in Lake Lednica, once linked to shore by two massive wooden bridges whose pilings remain visible underwater. The Museum of the First Piasts at Lednica documents the origins of the Polish state here, listed as a Historic Monument since 1969. Some historians place Mieszko I's baptism on this very spot in 966.

The cut stones of Mieszko I's pre-Romanesque palatium radiate a strange presence, lying there in the grass without dramatic staging. These 10th-century ruins rank among the best-preserved from medieval Poland. Head down to the excavations: a baptismal font discovered in the 1980s bolsters the theory that Poland's baptism happened right here.

activities.text: If you arrive late afternoon, the raking light over Lake Lednica from the island ramparts is particularly photogenic. The last ferry back departs at 6 PM in high season - time your return carefully. On Thursdays, museum admission is free.

days_plan.itin_title: Greater Poland: Birthplace of the Polish State in 9 Days

map_keywords: Museum of the First Piasts Lednica

visits.text: The Auschwitz-Birkenau memorial comprises two sites 3km apart: Auschwitz I (the former Polish camp converted into a concentration camp in 1940, now the main museum) and Auschwitz II-Birkenau (the largest extermination camp built from 1941). The Soviet Red Army liberated the site on 27 January 1945, a date since recognized as International Holocaust Remembrance Day. The guided tour lasts a minimum of 3.5 hours. Entry is free; guided tours are charged (approximately 100 PLN). Booking required at auschwitz.org, several weeks in advance during high season.

activities.text: Time for contemplation and personal reflection.
Visit to the historic centre of Oświęcim.

days_plan.itin_title: Kraków, Tatras & Memory: Southern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Żelazowa Wola lies 46 km west of Warsaw, on the Masovian plain. Frédéric Chopin was born there on March 1, 1810 - his parents left the village that same autumn. The birthplace, a former outbuilding of the Skarbek manor, has been transformed into a museum. The park, crossed by a stream and planted with willows, houses five Chopin monuments. Open-air piano recitals are held every Saturday and Sunday from May to September, at noon and 3 PM, included in the ticket. Closed Mondays.

Płock overlooks the Vistula from a 50-meter cliff. Its diocese was founded in 1075; from 1079 to 1138, the Dukes of Poland resided there, making it the country's political center. The Romanesque cathedral, built between 1130 and 1144 by Bishop Alexander of Malonne (from the Namur region), houses the tombs of Vladislaus I Herman and Bolesław III Wrymouth. Tumskie Hill (Wzgórze Tumskie) offers panoramic views over the Vistula and the flat Masovian lands.

activities.text: Kampinos National Park, adjacent to Żelazowa Wola, covers 385 km² of lowland forests between Warsaw and Żelazowa Wola - one of the rare national parks in the immediate vicinity of a European capital. Lynx populations have been reintroduced there. The time available between Warsaw and Żelazowa Wola allows for a brief walk on marked trails along the inland dunes.

days_plan.itin_title: Warsaw and Masovia: Capital, Chopin and the Vistula

visits.text: Thirty-five thousand bats can't be wrong: the tunnels of the Międzyrzecki Rejon Umocniony (MRU) offer Europe's largest winter bat refuge. These Nazi fortifications, built between 1934 and 1944 to protect the route to Berlin, total nearly 35 kilometers of underground galleries up to 40 meters deep. The Museum of Fortifications and Bats at Pniewo manages guided visits.

Built between 1934 and 1944, the MRU was Nazi Germany's most technologically advanced defensive system. It comprises a hundred concrete structures linked by a network of tunnels, with underground stations, workshops and barracks. The Soviets crossed it in three days in January 1945 - the German army hadn't had time to arm it. Military irony rarely so visible.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Fortifications and Bats at Pniewo (bunkry.pl): several circuits are offered depending on interest and fitness level, with optional headlamps. Bring a jacket even in summer - the underground temperature stays cool. The "Boryszyn loop" is the most accessible circuit for non-specialists.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: Since 1906, the Spa Park of Polanica-Zdrój has spread over 13 hectares in the very center—main promenade, gazebos, protected century-old rhododendrons. The spa town's history begins in 1828 with the first captures of the Józef spring. This is where Staropolanka is produced, a mineral water sold throughout Poland since the 1970s.

The water from the Wielka Pieniawa spring rises from 34 meters depth in the Art Nouveau Pump Room (1911–1913, architects Paul Rother and Felix Wild): the classicist building tinged with Jugendstil is the most elegant in the spa valley. A glass of this water charged with magnesium, calcium, and lithium leaves a persistent metallic taste. Open to all, free entry.

Polanica's Mineral Water Pump Room is probably the finest spa building in the Kotlina Kłodzka: pilasters, stained glass, stone colonnade, opus sectile floor. Restored after the 1998 floods, it regained its original state thanks to archival photographs. Inside too, the Art Nouveau basins are worth a look even without thirst.

activities.text: For medical treatments, Uzdrowisko Polanica-Zdrój offers more than 110 different procedures—mineral baths, mud, dry CO2, cryotherapy. For passing travelers, the Słoneczne Wielka Pieniawa Spa (thermal water park) is accessible without prescription: two heated pools, Finnish saunas, and hammam. Water park rate around 60–80 PLN/half-day.

Slide down the dry toboggan or lounge around the giant open-air chessboard in the park. The central promenade also hosts sculptures and a bandstand where concerts are held in summer. Bike rental available near the park's north entrance for riding along the Bystrzyca Dusznicka.

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone

visits.text: The smell of sulphur and peat greets visitors at Połczyn-Zdrój, a spa town since the discovery of mineral springs in 1688. The Park Zdrojowy, laid out in the 1830s, extends over about 80 hectares of old trees and period pavilions. Gentle atmosphere, far from the coast.

activities.text: Stroll through the Park Zdrojowy, laid out in the 19th century. The thermal drinking fountains still operate. If you're looking for treatment, the spa centres offer mud and brine therapies. Moderate rates for Poland.

days_plan.itin_title: West Pomerania: Szczecin, Baltic Sea & Lakes

visits.text: The carillon strikes noon: two mechanical goats emerge from the Town Hall tower and clash horns for twelve beats. Poznań's Old Market Square, remodeled in Renaissance style during the 16th century by architect Giovanni Battista di Quadro, ranks among Poland's most beautiful medieval squares. Arrive before 11:45 to claim a spot facing the tower and catch the caprine duel.

Poznań's Cathedral Island, Ostrów Tumski, is technically an island encircled by the Warta River's branches—this is where Poland was born. The Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul, rebuilt in Gothic style after several fires, houses the tombs of Poland's first rulers. Start at the Porta Posnania Centre on the opposite bank: its audio guide sets the scene before you step onto the island.

Porta Posnania looks like a glass pavilion planted on the river, but the building literally spans the canal between Śródka and Ostrów Tumski via a glass footbridge. Immersive exhibitions explain 1,000 years of Polish history without pompous text. Paid admission; allow 45 minutes.

Poznań Cathedral is austere from outside—barely two sober towers—but the crypt conceals something extraordinary: Romanesque foundations from the 10th century, the era of Poland's baptism in 966. It's one of the country's oldest Christian sites. Climb the tower for views across the island and the Warta's meander.

activities.text: You'll cross through Śródka to reach Ostrów Tumski and discover along the way the neighborhood's 3D mural fresco—one of Poland's most accomplished trompe-l'oeil. The walk from the Old Market Square takes twenty minutes. By tram, get off at Katedra stop.

Before leaving Poznań, stop by a bakery on the Old Market Square to try the St. Martin's croissant (Rogal Świętomarciński): puff pastry filled with white poppy seeds, a recipe protected since 2008. Expect to pay 15 to 20 PLN each. It exists only in Poznań, nowhere else in Poland.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland Complete Journey: Baltic Sea to Tatra Mountains Adventure
Greater Poland: Birthplace of the Polish State in 9 Days
Wrocław, Silesian Castles and the Sudetes in 12 Days
Poland: Lower Silesia and Greater Poland in 5 Days

visits.text: In 1801, Princess Izabela Czartoryska opened Poland's first museum in the Temple of the Sibyl at Puławy, a circular edifice inspired by the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli. The original collections are now in Kraków, but the building perched on the Vistula escarpment remains moving.

Cross the English-style park of Czartoryski Palace to reach the Gothic House (Dom Gotycki), Izabela's second museum pavilion, opened in 1809. The original Baroque palace (1671–1679) has been rebuilt several times. The park is free to access.

activities.text: From the Temple of the Sibyl's terrace, the view plunges onto the Vistula below. It's one of the region's finest viewpoints. Allow fifteen minutes to soak it in, especially late in the day.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: The Radków Rocks rise above the town of Radków like a movie backdrop: sandstone needles, mushroom rocks, and tableau-shaped cliffs dominate the Góry Stołowe plateau from the heights of the national park. From Radków's Rynek, the access trail climbs in 30 minutes. Free access—unlike the paid sites in the neighboring park.

Radków is modest, but its position as the northern gateway to the Góry Stołowe makes it an ideal base for hikers. The Gothic Church of St. Dorothy, one of the oldest in the region, can be visited in a few minutes. The market square surrounded by arcaded houses retains the quiet atmosphere of a border town forgotten by the main tourist routes.

activities.text: From Radków, take the Szosa Stu Zakrętów (Road of a Hundred Bends) to Karłów by car: 12 km of hairpins through the national park, with panoramas of sandstone formations at every turn. It's the most spectacular scenic route in the entire Kotlina Kłodzka. Closed to freight transport, open to cars.

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone

visits.text: Sure, the names are kitsch - but the trees themselves command respect. Lech, Czech, and Rus, the three legendary oaks in Rogalin Palace park, bear the names of the mythical founders of Poland, Bohemia, and Russia. Their circumferences reach 9.3, 8.1, and 6.7 meters respectively - measured periodically by botanists since 1904.

The blazing red of Jan Matejko's Joan of Arc occupies an entire wall of the gallery. Rogalin Palace, built between 1768 and 1815 for the Raczyński family, displays a curated selection of 19th-century Polish and European paintings. The French audio guide automatically detects which room you're in.

activities.text: Walking through the French formal garden then the English landscape park leads directly to the oak reserve along the Warta River. Wear solid shoes: the dirt paths turn muddy after rain. An hour is enough to reach the three famous oaks and circle back to the palace.

days_plan.itin_title: Greater Poland: Birthplace of the Polish State in 9 Days
Poland: Lower Silesia and Greater Poland in 5 Days

visits.text: Bukowa Góra is worth the detour for its two-hundred-year-old beeches and giant firs. The reserve, established in 1934 under the Zamoyski Ordination, covers 134 hectares of strictly protected forest. The green trail from Zwierzyniec education center runs 2.5 km with 80 m of elevation gain. Bring sturdy shoes for the wooden steps.

Stop at the summit for the view over the checkerboard fields of the Roztocze plateau, at 310 m altitude. A table and bench make for a good picnic spot. The descent toward the village of Sochy passes a cemetery commemorating the 1943 pacification of the village.

activities.text: The konik polski horses graze at Florianka, a former forestry estate converted into a breeding farm since 1995. These small gray horses, descendants of wild tarpans, stand barely 130 cm at the withers. Access is free, but stay behind the fences.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Rydzyna Castle, the Leszczyński family's Baroque palace, was the residence of the family of future King Stanisław. Raised in the late 17th century on medieval foundations, burned in 1945 then rebuilt between 1970 and 1989, it boasts a ballroom with painted ceiling. Today it houses a hotel and museum. Check hours—access sometimes depends on events.

Cross Rydzyna's market square, conceived as a model Baroque town around the castle. You'll see a Baroque town hall topped with a Rococo Trinity figure. The whole ensemble can be covered in fifteen minutes. Ideal for stretching your legs before Leszno, close by.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: Lower Silesia and Greater Poland in 5 Days

visits.text: Sandomierz gladly compares itself to Rome because it's built on seven hills. The ambition is out of proportion, but the medieval rynek with its Gothic town hall and arcaded houses delivers on its promises. Few foreign tourists, plenty of charm.

Opened in 1997, the Trasa Podziemna (Underground Route) connects 30 merchant cellars over 470 m beneath the Old Town. The deepest point descends to 12 m below the square. Guided tour in Polish every hour, about 40 minutes. Book ahead in summer.

Climb to the top of the Brama Opatowska, the only surviving medieval gate of the original four. The terrace offers a complete panorama over the rooftops, the Vistula and the meander. A few zlotys entry.

The Gothic cathedral conceals Russo-Byzantine frescoes in its interior remodeled in Baroque in the seventeenth century. The crypt merits a look. Next door, the Dom Długosza (1476) was the house of Poland's first historian, Jan Długosz. Small diocesan museum inside.

activities.text: If the heat rises, the brine graduation tower (tężnia solankowa) in the park offers a refreshing iodized mist. It's free and locals come to breathe as if at the seaside.

Fans of the TV series Ojciec Mateusz (Father Matthew) will recognize Sandomierz: it's the main setting of this ecclesiastical crime drama hugely popular in Poland. The exteriors are shot on the rynek and in the alleyways.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days
Poland: From Wrocław to the Holy Cross Mountains in 5 Days

visits.text: The Sanok skansen is Poland's largest open-air folk architecture museum and Europe's second largest, with over 180 wooden buildings across 38 hectares. The structures were moved from their original villages, restored to their initial state, and recreate four distinct ethnographic worlds: Boyko and Lemko villages in the upper section, Pogórzanie and Doliniane lower down. Each sector has its period Orthodox or Greek Catholic church, its period-furnished houses, its forges and water mills.

Sanok's Royal Castle, dominating the town from a hilltop, houses a collection of Byzantine and Greek Catholic icons from the Carpathians, one of Poland's most important. An entire room is devoted to the work of Zdzisław Beksiński (1929-2005), a painter born in Sanok, known for his hallucinatory post-apocalyptic landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Enter the magical world of Hobbit Village in Sieraków Sławieński, a unique attraction inspired by the fantastical universe of J.R.R. Tolkien's beloved novels. Children can explore authentic-looking hobbit houses built into the hillside, participate in outdoor games and join creative handicraft workshops. Themed parties, picnics and bonfires create an enchanting atmosphere that stimulates imagination and adventure.

Visit Pomerania Amusement Park in nearby Dygowo, offering attractions for children of all ages from gentle carousels to exciting rides. The park features trains, slides, a rope course and bouncy castles alongside a dedicated toddler zone with safe, age-appropriate play equipment. Organized animations, competitions and artistic performances ensure non-stop entertainment throughout the day.

activities.text: Dress comfortably for outdoor adventures in the Hobbit Village and prepare for field games and creative activities

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast

visits.text: Listed on the Ramsar list since 1984, Ujście Warty National Park protects the wetlands where the Warta flows into the Odra. 270 bird species have been recorded here, including 174 breeding ones - among them the white-tailed eagle, black stork and 26 globally threatened species. The park calls itself the "Republic of Birds."

At dawn, cranes descend by the thousands onto flooded meadows in autumn - up to 20,000 individuals recorded during migration. In October-November, tundra and taiga geese accumulate by the hundreds of thousands. All from the Chyrzyno observation tower, next to park headquarters. Bring binoculars.

activities.text: Hop on a bike to cover the Barnacle Goose trail - 21 kilometers of flat tracks between dikes and canals, with stops at observation posts. Maps available at Stacja Pomp 2 in Słońsk. Bike access possible from Kostrzyn. In summer, woolly sheep and Hucul horses graze freely in the polders.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: Sopot's pier extends 511.5 meters into the Baltic Sea. The first structure was built in 1827 by Dr. Jerzy Haffner; the pier reached its current length through successive expansions. It's the longest wooden pier in Europe. In season, access is ticketed. Views stretch to Gdańsk and Gdynia from the far end.

The Crooked House (Krzywy Domek, 2004), at 53 Monte Cassino Street, is a commercial building whose undulating facade was inspired by the illustrations of Polish storyteller and artist Jan Marcin Szancer. It's part of the Rezydent shopping center. The surreal architecture contrasts with the Belle Époque and Art Nouveau villas along the main boulevard.

activities.text: The pedestrian street Bohaterów Monte Cassino, linking the train station to the sea, concentrates boutiques, restaurants, and terraces. Sopot has been the seaside resort of aristocracy and haute bourgeoisie since the late 19th century—Germany's Wilhelm II and numerous European sovereigns vacationed here. The Grand Hotel (1927, neoclassical style) borders the beach north of the pier. The Forest Opera (Opera Leśna), set in the woods behind the town, hosts open-air concerts every summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
Polish Baltic Coast: Gdańsk, Sopot & the Teutonic Fortress

visits.text: Never conquered—even Napoleon had to give up taking Srebrna Góra Fortress in 1807. This mountain fortress, considered the largest in Europe, looms over the village from its three summits. The central Donjon could house 4,000 soldiers in 151 rooms with walls up to 12 meters thick in places. Guides in Napoleonic soldier costumes add life to the visit.

Built between 1765 and 1777 by order of Frederick II of Prussia, Srebrna Góra Fortress was meant to protect Prussian Silesia against Austria. Engineer Ludwig Wilhelm Regler's plans were amended by the king himself. It has been a Polish National Monument since 2004 and is a candidate for UNESCO inscription in a transnational dossier with Germany's Königstein and Italy's Fenestrelle.

activities.text: Visit the Donjon on a guided tour—the only way to access the bombproof casemates and the 151 rooms from the 18th century. In summer on weekends, the Fort Fan Express ski lift connects Srebrna Góra pass to the fort. Admission around 30–40 PLN; check opening times beforehand (closed outside tourist season).

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone
Wrocław, Silesian Castles and the Sudetes in 12 Days

visits.text: Poles call it 'the little cathedral' even though it's a collegiate church. Stargard's Kolegiata Mariacka, begun in 1292, was inspired by Lübeck's Marienkirche. Red bricks, guild chapels and 15th-century polychromy inside. National heritage monument since 2010.

Less well-known than the collegiate church, the Willa Karowów blends Art Nouveau and neo-Gothic on a cobbled street. It's not open to visitors, but the façade is worth a detour for architecture fans. Two-minute walk from the Rynek.

Built in the 13th and 14th centuries, Stargard's mury obronne (defensive walls) are on the European Brick Gothic route. Several towers still stand, including the Witches' Tower. The pedestrian circuit is signposted and about 2 km long.

activities.text: Walk around the ramparts in an hour to grasp the scale of the fortified old town. Plentiful free parking in Stargard, a rare luxury in Poland.

days_plan.itin_title: West Pomerania: Szczecin, Baltic Sea & Lakes

visits.text: Śnieżnik at 1,425 m is the highest peak of the eastern Polish Sudetes—and the view from the observation tower confirms the obvious: Śnieżka and the Karkonosze to the northwest, the Czech Jeseníky to the south, the Kotlina Kłodzka reliefs all around. An honest effort for a rare visual reward. Ascent from Sienna via the red trail: 2.5 hours one way.

From the summit of Śnieżnik, in clear weather, you can spot Śnieżka—the roof of the Sudetes at 1,602 m—more than 80 kilometers away. The massif forms the watershed between the Vistula, Oder, and Danube basins. The Polish-Czech border runs exactly through the summit. The observation tower at the top is accessible year-round.

activities.text: Ride the gondola from Czarna Góra Resort to the summit of Czarna Góra (1,205 m), then continue on foot via the red trail to Śnieżnik (1,425 m)—2 hours walking through beech forest. In winter, the resort offers 20 slopes and Poland's fastest 6-person gondola. Parking at the resort, paid in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone

visits.text: Tykocin, on the route between Białystok and Supraśl, warrants an hour's stop. Its baroque synagogue of 1642—one of Poland's best preserved—stands on a square virtually unchanged for three centuries. Inside, Hebrew inscriptions cover the walls from floor to ceiling. The contrast between this silence and the bustle of modern Białystok is striking.

In Supraśl, the Monastery of the Annunciation was founded in 1498 by Marshal of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania Aleksander Chodkiewicz. The main church, built between 1503 and 1511, was destroyed by the German army in 1944 and reconstructed over 40 years—it wasn't consecrated again until 2021. The Palace of the Archimandrites (1635-1655) houses the Icon Museum with some 400 pieces. The baroque buildings from the 17th-18th centuries remain in monastic use.

activities.text: The village of Supraśl is surrounded by the Knyszyn Forest. A bike ride or walk on the marked forest trails offers a gradual introduction to the atmosphere of Podlachia's great forests, before tomorrow's primeval forest of Białowieża.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: The 17th-century organ resonates in Świdnica's Church of Peace with a clarity that astonishes in a wooden building. Built between 1656 and 1658, this church is Europe's largest Baroque Protestant timber church. Its sister in Jawor feels more rural; Świdnica is more ornate, more restored. UNESCO-listed in 2001 alongside Jawor. The acoustics explain why the International Bach Festival has been held here annually since 2000.

Świdnica's Market Square is one of Lower Silesia's best-preserved, with its arcaded houses and Gothic Town Hall. Take ten minutes to walk the full perimeter—the town is human-scale and most cafés open directly onto it. Good lunch stop before heading to Książ.

activities.text: If you pass through in July, the Świdnica Bach Festival programs organ concerts in the church itself—best seats book out several weeks in advance. Outside the festival, occasional concerts happen Saturday afternoons. Check the schedule at kosciolpokoju.pl.

days_plan.itin_title: Wrocław, Silesian Castles and the Sudetes in 12 Days
Poland: Lower Silesia and Greater Poland in 5 Days

visits.text: Kitsch for some, stunning for all: the Pomnik Chrystusa Króla of Świebodzin is Guinness-certified as the world's tallest Jesus statue in 2010 - a ranking contested since 2015 by other achievements. It measures 33 meters (the height symbolizes Christ's age at death), 52.5 meters with its mound and golden crown. It's taller than Rio's Christ the Redeemer by 14.5 meters. Inaugurated in November 2010, financed by donations from the town's 21,000 inhabitants.

Walk the full circuit of the platform to grasp the proportions: each hand measures 6 meters, the wingspan is 24 meters from fingertip to fingertip. The statue weighs 440 tons in total. The resemblance to Christ the Redeemer is intentional - the superiority in height even more so. Amusing or pious depending on your sensibility, but hard to ignore.

activities.text: The 16th-century town hall and medieval ramparts of downtown Świebodzin deserve half an hour after the statue. The old town is quiet and unpretentious - a good counterbalance to the neighboring religious gigantism. On the main square, a few good cafes for lunch before hitting the road again.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: The oldest sanctuary in Poland demands an effort: the Holy Cross Abbey on Łysa Góra (595 m) has kept, since the fourteenth century, relics of the True Cross that gave their name to the entire region. The summit was also a site of Slavic pagan worship before the Benedictines arrived. The final approach is on foot via a road closed to traffic; be ready for the climb.

activities.text: Walk to the platform overlooking the gołoborza, those boulder fields typical of the Holy Cross Mountains, the most extensive in Poland. All around, the fir forest of Świętokrzyski National Park shelters a natural-history museum installed in one wing of the abbey. The trail crosses a reserve: stay on the marked boardwalks.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: From Wrocław to the Holy Cross Mountains in 5 Days

visits.text: Discover the Świdwie Nature Reserve, a paradise for birdwatchers and nature enthusiasts located in pristine wetlands near Szczecin. This protected area is home to remarkable bird species including cranes, storks and majestic white-tailed eagles that nest in the marshlands. Educational trails and observation towers provide comfortable viewing platforms to witness these magnificent creatures in their natural habitat.

Ascend the Świnoujście Lighthouse, the tallest lighthouse on the entire Polish coast and one of the highest in the world. The climb to the top presents a rewarding challenge, with breathtaking panoramic views of the city, the bustling port and the endless Baltic Sea as your prize. This iconic maritime beacon has guided ships safely into harbor for over 160 years.

Step back in time at Fort Gerhard, where interactive tours led by guides in authentic 19th-century soldier costumes bring military history vividly to life. This well-preserved fortress offers a fascinating glimpse into the strategic importance of this coastal location and the soldiers who once defended it. The tunnels, ramparts and gun emplacements create an adventurous atmosphere perfect for young explorers.

Visit Lake Turquoise in nearby Wapnica, a stunning reservoir with intensely turquoise-colored water created on the site of a former chalk mine. This picturesque lake offers walking paths through extraordinary landscapes where the vibrant water colors create an almost surreal natural spectacle. Viewpoints and picnic areas provide perfect spots to absorb the unique beauty of this geological wonder.

activities.text: Bring binoculars and cameras to capture the diverse wetland ecosystems and learn about the ecological importance of these protected marshes
Walk along the scenic trails surrounding Lake Turquoise to capture unique photographs of its remarkable turquoise waters

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
West Pomerania: Szczecin, Baltic Sea & Lakes

visits.text: Poles know Szczebrzeszyn for an impossible tongue-twister ('w Szczebrzeszynie chrząszcz brzmi w trzcinie'). A two-meter beetle has stood on the rynek since 2011 to celebrate Jan Brzechwa's poem. Photo stop mandatory.

The Renaissance synagogue, remodeled in Baroque style in the eighteenth century, rises on Sadowa street, recognizable by its broken roof and arcade frieze. A first bożnica existed as early as 1584; destroyed during the Chmielnicki wars (1648–49), it was rebuilt after 1659. Today it houses the municipal cultural center. Behind it, a path leads to the Jewish cemetery overgrown by forest, where a monument recalls the massacre and deportation of 1942.

Climb Wzgórze Zamkowe (Castle Hill) for a panorama over the Wieprz valley and Roztocze. The site has been inhabited for thousands of years. Nothing remains of the castle, but the view justifies the ten-minute climb.

activities.text: Founded in 1394 by Dymitr of Goraj, the kościół św. Mikołaja is the town's oldest church. Rebuilt between 1610 and 1620 in Lublin Renaissance style, it preserves an atmosphere and stucco work close to Zamość cathedral. Free entry.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Explore the magnificent Szczecin Castle, the Renaissance-style former residence of the Dukes of Pomerania that dominates the city's skyline. This impressive fortress complex houses museums, concert halls and courtyards that tell the story of the region's noble heritage. The castle's towers offer panoramic views over the Oder River and the surrounding historic quarter.

Marvel at St. Jacob's Cathedral, one of the largest brick Gothic churches in the entire region, whose soaring spire was only recently restored to its former glory. The cathedral's imposing interior features magnificent vaulted ceilings and medieval artwork that showcase the architectural prowess of Hanseatic craftsmen. This sacred space has witnessed centuries of prayer and devotion since the Middle Ages.

Admire the award-winning Philharmonic Hall, a stunning contemporary building that opened in 2015 and has garnered international acclaim for both its architecture and exceptional acoustics. The building's angular white facade creates a striking contrast with the city's historic structures and represents Poland's modern cultural renaissance. Concert-goers from across Europe come to experience performances in this acoustic masterpiece.

Stroll along the Hakenterrasse (Wały Chrobrego), a magnificent ensemble of early 20th-century buildings perched above the Oder River. This elegant promenade offers spectacular views of the waterfront, the port and the ships passing by below. The terraced gardens and monumental architecture create one of the most photographed panoramas in all of Pomerania.

activities.text: Challenge yourself at Tarzania Rope Park, where various courses with different difficulty levels allow visitors of all ages to test their agility among the treetops
Visit the Museum of Technology and Communication to discover vintage cars, motorcycles, trams and historic computing equipment with hands-on interactive exhibitions

days_plan.itin_title: Baltic Coast Family Road Trip: Adventures on Poland's Pomeranian Coast
West Pomerania: Szczecin, Baltic Sea & Lakes

places.name: Szczytna

visits.text: This 22-hectare labyrinth in the Góry Stołowe looks like nothing natural—yet it's entirely so. The Błędne Skały (Errant Rocks) are Cretaceous sandstone formations sculpted by erosion at 853 m altitude in the Table Mountains National Park. Entry ticket required, with online booking in season.

In the 17th century, the Błędne Skały served as a smuggling route between Bohemia and Prussian Silesia: inscriptions carved into the smooth sandstone still commemorate arrest dates. The route is one-way over 1.5 km, impossible to turn back as the passages are too narrow. Duration 40 minutes. Bring shoes with non-slip soles.

activities.text: Take the one-way circuit of Błędne Skały early in the morning to avoid human traffic jams in the narrowest passages. The upper parking (limited) is only open in season; the lower parking (free) requires an additional 4 km on foot. In July–August, the wait can exceed 2 hours. Online ticket advised at pngs.gov.pl.

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone

map_keywords: Błędne Skały Table Mountains National Park

visits.text: The spray from Kamieńczyk Waterfall reaches you before you see it: the cascade drops in three tiers from 27 meters, the highest in the Polish part of the Karkonosze. The Kamieńczyk Gorge that receives it measures only 4 meters wide in places. Director Andrew Adamson shot scenes from Prince Caspian (2008) here for its fairy-tale settings. Open since 1890.

activities.text: Kamieńczyk Gorge, a 100-meter-long gorge with rock walls over 25 meters high, descends via a narrow staircase from an upper observation platform. Paid admission to descend into the gorge (helmet provided). In winter, the waterfall freezes and creates spectacular ice formations.

days_plan.itin_title: Wrocław, Silesian Castles and the Sudetes in 12 Days

visits.text: Eleven hundred inhabitants, but ramparts 1,080 m long in local limestone, 1.8 m thick, dating from the fourteenth century. Szydłów is nicknamed the 'Polish Carcassonne,' and for once the nickname doesn't lie. The medieval layout is intact.

Founded around 1534–1564, the late Gothic synagogue has survived wars and today serves as the municipal cultural center. Recent restoration (EU-funded) has brought the building back to life. Exhibition on local Jewish history inside.

Pass through the Kraków Gate (Brama Krakowska), the only one of the three medieval gates still standing. It guarded the southern entrance to the town. The neighboring royal castle, remodeled under Władysław Jagiełło, houses a multimedia exhibition on local history.

activities.text: In late August, Szydłów celebrates the Plum Festival (Festiwal Śliwki): the region is a fruit-growing basin. Drying demonstrations, tastings of śliwowica (plum brandy) and local jams. Outside the festival, the orchards are still beautiful in spring.

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Toruń is one of the rare medieval town centers in Poland to have survived the 20th century intact. The Old Town, inscribed on UNESCO's list in 1997, preserves its red Gothic brick since the 13th century - you walk its cobbled streets with the feeling of having time-traveled effortlessly. Arrive in the evening for the light on the façades.

Nicolaus Copernicus was born in this house in the Old Town in 1473. Copernicus House, a museum housed in two adjoining medieval townhouses, displays astronomical instruments and traces the heliocentric revolution. The statue of the scholar on the square bears the inscription: 'he stopped the sun and set the earth in motion.'

Toruń's Gothic town hall - the Old Town Hall - looks like an austere monument; inside, it's one of Poland's richest regional museums, with Gothic sculptures and Hanseatic silverware. The tower offers a panorama over the Vistula that chronicler Jan Długosz already described in the 15th century as unmatched in beauty. Count on 200 steps.

activities.text: Book a session at Toruń's Gingerbread Museum to knead and decorate your own gingerbread following the town's medieval recipe. The visit lasts about an hour - tasting included. Reserve online: slots often sell out in summer.

The scent of moss on the Teutonic ramparts traces the Vistula shore in rare silence. The ruins of the Teutonic Order castle - deliberately razed by Toruń's burghers in 1454 - stretch several hundred meters along the riverbank. Thirty minutes of free wandering is enough to see it all.

visits.text: Founded in 1202 by Duke Henry the Bearded and his wife Hedwig, the Basilica of St. Hedwig in Trzebnica housed Poland's first women's convent. Late Romanesque style touched up by Baroque, it preserves the tombs of St. Hedwig and her husband, along with paintings by Michael Willmann. A twenty-minute stop suffices. Proper attire required—the place remains an active sanctuary.

days_plan.itin_title: Poland: Lower Silesia and Greater Poland in 5 Days

visits.text: A single wall still stands, perched on the edge of a sand cliff. The Gothic church of St Nicholas in Trzęsacz was built in the 14th-15th centuries, nearly 2 km from the sea. Erosion did the rest. In 1994, half of the south wall collapsed; what remains is stabilized on gabions. Unique case in Europe.

activities.text: Stop by the multimedia museum on the cliff (Muzeum na Klifie), four interactive rooms explaining the phenomenon of coastal abrasion. Small but well done, ideal in rainy weather. Modest admission.

days_plan.itin_title: West Pomerania: Szczecin, Baltic Sea & Lakes

visits.text: Krzyżtopór is Poland's most beautiful ruin, and that's no overstatement. Built between the 1620s and 1644 by Krzysztof Ossoliński, this pentagonal palazzo in fortezza surpassed Versailles in size before Versailles even existed. Legend has it the castle had as many windows as days in the year. The Swedes pillaged it in 1655, the Russians finished it off in 1770. Since then, no one has had the means to restore it.

activities.text: If you love ruins, take time to climb the turrets still accessible. The view over the Świętokrzyskie countryside is unobstructed. The site is managed by the commune of Iwaniska, paid entry (a few zlotys).

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Wałbrzych locals call Książ the castle of Princess Daisy—named for the last lady of the house before 1943, Marie-Thérèse de Cornwallis-West, a British aristocrat whose memoirs caused scandal. Built between 1288 and 1292 by Bolko I, later expanded, Książ is Poland's third-largest castle. Prisoners from Gross-Rosen camp dug tunnels at 15 and 50 meters depth during World War II—their precise purpose remains mysterious.

You'll descend fifteen meters beneath the castle into the WWII tunnels called Riese (Giant): bare concrete, right-angled galleries, no decoration—the impression is radically different from the Baroque halls above. Book the combined castle+tunnels ticket. The guided underground tour lasts an additional 45 minutes.

activities.text: In Książ park's French gardens, the scent of roses in June is strong enough to divert attention from the castle. The adjacent palm house, a gift from Hans Heinrich XV to Princess Daisy, was bought by the city of Wałbrzych and restored. Allow 30 minutes for the park alone—a good way to decompress after the tunnels.

days_plan.itin_title: Wrocław, Silesian Castles and the Sudetes in 12 Days

visits.text: The Basilica of the Visitation in Wambierzyce conceals architectural grandeur behind a 50-meter-high facade: the Baroque interior, built between 1715 and 1723, opens onto a nave taller than it appears from the forecourt. The 56 steps of the monumental staircase each carry liturgical significance—the central 33 symbolize Christ's years. Free entry daily 7am to 7pm.

You'll climb Wambierzyce's Calvary via the 79 stations scattered across the hill facing the basilica—the entire town was redesigned in the 18th century on the model of Jerusalem, with 14 gates and a stream named Kidron. The ascent takes about 45 minutes and offers views of the Góry Stołowe (Table Mountains) in the background.

activities.text: Poles call it Silesian Jerusalem—and the Szopka, Wambierzyce's animated nativity scene from the second half of the 19th century, reinforces the analogy with 79 mechanical figures in motion. Visit it in the building at the foot of the Calvary, open 9am to 6pm (off-season: 10am-4pm). Paid admission, separate from the basilica.

places.name: Warsaw

visits.text: The Blikle pastry shop (Nowy Świat 33) was founded by Antoni Blikle in 1869. It never closed—not even during the German occupation, which earned it, according to family tradition, the tacit protection of German and Soviet officers who appreciated its pączki. Charles de Gaulle, who had lodged above the pastry shop during his stay in Warsaw in 1919-1920, regularly ordered pączki via the French diplomatic pouch during his presidential visits. The house pączki is filled with rose or rosehip jam, fried in lard, dusted with powdered sugar, and finished with a hint of orange zest.

Bar Bambino or Bar Prasowy: the two best milk bars (bars mleczny) in Warsaw. These state-subsidized canteens, subsidized since the socialist era, serve dishes for 15-25 PLN (€3-6) in formica décor unchanged since the 1960s. Żurek in a deep bowl, boiled pierogi with fried onions, gołąbki (stuffed cabbage), prune kompot. Lines are short in the morning, long between noon and 2 p.m. No reservations, no tips, no cards.

The Polish Vodka Museum is housed in the former Koneser distillery, built in 1897 in the Praga district. The guided tour (70-80 minutes) covers five interactive galleries: the medieval alchemist who used vodka as a remedy, the 18th-century noble court, 19th-century industrialization, and communism. It concludes with a tasting of three to four vodkas at the Vodka Academy Bar. Online booking required. Standard rate: about 70 PLN (2024) all-inclusive.

Explore Warsaw's meticulously reconstructed Old Town, a UNESCO World Heritage site that stands as a remarkable testament to Polish resilience. After sustaining over 85% destruction during World War II, the city was patiently rebuilt by its determined citizens. The charming market square and surrounding streets now present the architectural beauty that once characterized this historic capital.

Visit the Royal Castle, the former official residence of Polish monarchs beautifully restored to its Baroque splendor. This grand palace overlooking Castle Square contains sumptuously decorated state rooms, royal apartments, and an impressive collection of paintings and decorative arts. The castle serves both as a museum and a symbol of Poland's royal heritage.

Discover the Warsaw Uprising Museum, an innovative and deeply moving tribute to the 1944 uprising against Nazi occupation. Through immersive exhibits, authentic artifacts, and multimedia presentations, this award-winning museum tells the story of the 63-day struggle that shaped modern Polish identity. The experience provides essential context for understanding Warsaw's wartime destruction and subsequent rebirth.

Stroll through Łazienki Park, Warsaw's largest and most beautiful park, featuring the enchanting Palace on the Water reflected in tranquil waters. This 18th-century royal summer residence, surrounded by manicured gardens, peacocks, and classical sculptures, offers a serene escape from urban exploration. The park's romantic landscapes and neoclassical architecture make it a favorite among locals and visitors alike.

Visit Wilanów Palace, often called the Polish Versailles, an exquisite Baroque royal residence set amid formal gardens on the city's outskirts. Built as a summer retreat for King John III Sobieski, the palace presents opulent interiors, period furnishings, and a remarkable collection of European art. The surrounding Italian and English gardens provide a picturesque setting for tranquil walks.

activities.text: Hala Mirowska, a stone's throw from Mirów Square: a red-brick covered market from the late 19th century where stalls of regional charcuterie, smoked fish, dried mushrooms, and artisan cheeses coexist with craft butchers. This is the daily supply source for some of Warsaw's chefs. An hour of browsing is enough to compare regional versions of kiełbasa (sausage) and identify the producers who supply the city center's fine-dining restaurants.

Comparative Polish vodka workshop: rye (żytnia—slightly harsh, bread note), wheat (pszeniczna—smoother, slightly floral), potato (ziemniaczana—round, persistent), bison grass (żubrówka—grassy, cut hay note), honey (krupnik—warm, spiced). A wine merchant or the museum bar offers this tasting format with explanations of raw materials and distillation methods. Duration: 60-90 minutes, glass of water between each.

Dinner at a new Polish cuisine restaurant—Nolita or Bez Gwiazdek are the two addresses pushing the boundaries of tradition without betraying it. Chefs work barszcz (beet broth) in different textures, carp as gravlax, fermented rye bread with hazelnut browned butter. Five- to seven-course tasting menus run around 200-280 PLN per person excluding pairings.

Food tour in the Praga district: beyond the vodka museum, this right-bank neighborhood retains a pre-war atmosphere with its cobbled courtyards and small shops. Bazar Różyckiego, one of Warsaw's oldest outdoor markets (operating since the 1880s), offers products unavailable elsewhere: rye-fermented pickles in wooden barrels, charcuterie from Podlachie producers, linden and buckwheat honeys. Two to three hours to wander the aisles with a guide or at your own pace.

Vodka bar crawl on Nowy Świat and Mazowiecka streets: vodka-pickle (the lacto-fermented pickle neutralizes the alcohol and cleanses the palate), house nalewka (maceration of cherries, quinces, or plums in neutral alcohol), Warsaw craft beer. This isn't an invitation to get wasted—the format works best in a small group with a roadmap. Several local tour operators offer this circuit with explanations of food-alcohol pairings.

visits.text: Centuries-old roots burst from the loess walls like giant veins: the wąwóz Korzeniowy Dół, about 400 m long, is the most photographed ravine around Kazimierz. Erosion has carved the soft rock into a narrow cleft where light filters between the trunks. Entry 4 PLN, parking 5 PLN/hr.

activities.text: Allow twenty minutes to walk through the bottom of the ravine one way. The ground can be slippery after rain—stay on the stabilized path. At the exit, a refreshment stand serves artisanal organic lemonades (linden, hawthorn).

days_plan.itin_title: From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: The scent of craft beer floats across Wrocław's Market Square as evening falls: this 13th-century Gothic square, enlarged during the Renaissance, concentrates several centuries of colorful facades. The Town Hall's belfry houses the Historical Museum of Wrocław. Look for Papa Krasnal in front of no. 5 on Świdnicka Street—it's the city's first dwarf, which appeared in 2001.

Centennial Hall, designed by Max Berg in 1913 and UNESCO-listed in 2006, supports a concrete dome 65 meters in diameter—at the time of construction, one of the world's largest. The Visitor Centre offers a virtual reality tour from the roof. Check the calendar before coming: the hall regularly hosts concerts that close it to tours.

Pope John Paul II said of Cathedral Island: 'where Poland began.' This Wrocław island, encircled by the Oder's branches, shelters one of the country's oldest cathedrals and 99 gas lamps lit by hand each evening. The atmosphere shifts dramatically after nightfall—the yellow flames pulse gently in the Oder's mist.

Tumski Bridge is Poland's only remaining gas-lit bridge—and one of only two in the entire European Union. Hundreds of lovers' padlocks hang there; the city removes them regularly to protect the structure. A bronze dwarf stands guard on each side: the Lovers' Dwarfs, officially installed to divert padlocks toward a nearby bench.

activities.text: You'll embark on a hunt for the 800 krasnale (bronze dwarfs) scattered across Wrocław since 2005—born from a peaceful resistance movement against communism in the 1980s, the idea conquered the entire city. Download the Wrocławskie Krasnale app or buy a map at the Market Square tourist office. One hour is enough to spot a dozen.

The multimedia fountain show resembles provincial sound-and-light—until water jets shoot 20 meters high synchronized to Beethoven or Queen. The Pergola adjoins Centennial Hall in Szczytnicki Park. Shows run evenings April through October; schedule at halastulecia.pl.

If you return in the evening, the lamplighter—one of only two still active in the EU—walks Cathedral Island in black cape and top hat, 30 minutes before sunset (4pm in winter, 9pm in summer). Watching him light the 99 gas lamps over 3.5 km has become a local ritual. No reservation required.

visits.text: The movies lie, history doesn't: the real Stalag Luft III at Żagań bears little resemblance to John Sturges' film, but the thrill is intact. On the night of March 24-25, 1944, 76 Allied prisoners escaped through tunnel Harry, dug 9 meters deep over 110 meters. Only three reached a neutral country; 50 were shot on Hitler's orders. The Muzeum Obozów Jenieckich reconstructs the camp with sobriety.

Paving stones engraved in the ground trace the underground route of tunnel Harry, bearing the names of the 76 escapees - those executed are in black letters. A reconstructed guard tower and a section of barbed wire restore the scale of the camp. The tunnel entrance and exit are marked by monuments. Allow at least 1 hour 30 minutes. Closed Mondays.

The silence of Żagań's baroque Palace contrasts sharply with the agitation of the neighboring site. Built for the Dukes of Sagan, it was inhabited in the 19th century by Duchess Dorothée de Talleyrand-Périgord, niece of the famous diplomat. The baroque interior is partially accessible; the gardens are freely open. From the library of the adjoining Augustinian monastery, globes and incunabula linked to astronomer Johannes Kepler are preserved.

activities.text: Walk along the paving stones that trace the 110 meters of tunnel Harry, read each name engraved in the ground. It's sober and effective, far more than any interactive museum. Continue to the British military cemetery in Citadel Park: white stones aligned under the pines, heavy and dignified atmosphere. Free entry.

The British military cemetery, in the quiet of Citadel Park, deserves 30 minutes of meditation after the chaos of the Stalag. We too often forget that Żagań was also a detention center during World War I. The Wieża Kościoła (evangelical church brick tower) downtown offers a rooftop view - check with the tourist office for access hours.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: Krupówki Street is Zakopane's main artery. The wooden architecture in "Zakopane style" lining it was theorized in the late 19th century by Stanisław Witkiewicz, who sought a national aesthetic rooted in góral (highlander) traditions. The wooden church at Jaszczurówka (1904-1907), designed by Witkiewicz himself, stands as the finest example of this style. The adjacent market sells oscypek - smoked sheep's milk cheese from pine wood, protected by European PDO since 2008.

Arrive in Zakopane, Poland's premier mountain resort nestled at the foot of the spectacular Tatra Mountains. This charming town, known as Poland's winter capital, enchants visitors with its distinctive wooden architecture, vibrant highland culture and breathtaking alpine scenery. The pedestrian main street lined with traditional timber buildings showcases the unique regional style that developed in this isolated mountain community.

Ride the funicular railway up Mount Gubałówka for breathtaking panoramic views across Zakopane and the dramatic Tatra peaks beyond. The summit ridge offers easy walking trails, souvenir stalls selling local crafts, and traditional mountain food to sample while taking in the mountain panoramas. On clear days, the jagged silhouette of the High Tatras creates an unforgettable alpine backdrop.

Hike to Morskie Oko, Poland's largest and most beautiful mountain lake, set in a glacial cirque surrounded by towering granite peaks. The 7.7-kilometre trail through Tatra National Park passes cascading streams and virgin pine forests before revealing the crystalline waters reflecting sheer rock walls. This iconic natural wonder, whose name means 'Eye of the Sea', represents the quintessential Tatra experience.

activities.text: The thermal baths aren't located in Zakopane itself but in the surrounding area. Termy Gorący Potok in Szaflary (12km) and the complexes at Bukowina Tatrzańska (20km) offer geothermal pools at 38-40°C, outdoors facing the Tatras. These facilities work best for late afternoon after the drive from Oświęcim. Allow 2 to 3 hours on site.

The Kasprowy Wierch cable car departs from Kuźnice (15 minutes from Zakopane centre) and climbs to 1,987m in two sections, changing at Myślenickie Turnie (1,354m). Total journey time is approximately 20 minutes. The summit station marks the border with Slovakia; in clear weather, the panorama encompasses the High Tatras on the Slovak side and the Podhale plain on the Polish side. From the summit, one recommended option is to walk down through Gąsienicowa Valley to Kuźnice (7.4km, 3h15, -1,000m descent, moderate trail). Cable car tickets are sold online at kolejkalinowa.pl and sell out fast in July-August. Expect temperatures 10-15°C lower than in town.

Alternative for those preferring to avoid elevation gain: Morskie Oko, the highest lake in the Polish Tatras (1,395m). The walk from Palenica Białczańska car park takes 2.5 hours each way (9km on paved road without technical climbing). The lake is surrounded by glacial cirques and rock walls. During high season, book parking online or arrive before 8am.

visits.text: Every house in Zalipie is repainted each spring before Corpus Christi. The tradition dates back to the late 19th century: village women used whitewash to cover soot stains left by old chimneyless stoves, then gradually transformed this practical gesture into increasingly complex floral patterns. Today, some thirty houses, barns, wells and dog kennels are covered with floral compositions—in blue, red and white on a white background.

The farm of Felicja Curyłowa (1904-1974) is open to the public as a museum. This artist, central figure of the tradition, had painted every surface of her three-room house—walls, ceiling, furniture, crockery and textiles included. Her first known work dates from 1914, when she was ten years old. After her death, the Cepelia Foundation bought the farm and preserved it identically.

activities.text: The House of Painters (Dom Malarek) offers workshops on floral painting on wood or ceramics, open to visitors without reservation. It's also the right place to get a village map and spot the best-decorated houses. The annual Malowana Chata ('painted cottage') competition, which has existed since 1948, takes place on the weekend following Corpus Christi, usually in early June.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage

visits.text: Zamość's Rynek Wielki is one of central Europe's finest squares, and the emptiness around proves it: no crowds, no tour buses. The colored arcades of Armenian merchant houses frame a town hall with fan-shaped stairs. Take time to sit at a terrace.

The Ratusz's fan-shaped staircase leads to a 52 m clock tower added in the eighteenth century. The original building, designed by Bernardo Morando, dates from the late sixteenth century. Tourist office on the ground floor, paid climb (a few zlotys).

A synagogue without worshippers, but among the best-preserved late Renaissance ones in Poland. Built between 1610 and 1618 at the initiative of Zamość's Sephardic community, it houses a small museum on local Jewish life. Before the war, the Jewish community made up nearly half the population.

Walk along the southern bastions to grasp the scale of the fortress that Tsar Alexander II had dismantled in the nineteenth century. Defensive walls remain for 2.5 km. The Arsenal Museum, installed in a former military warehouse, fills in the picture.

activities.text: If the weather allows, do the full circuit of the fortifications on foot. The walk is about 2.5 km and offers angles on the city you won't see from the square. Free, passable year-round.

Designed in 1578 by Bernardo Morando for Chancellor Jan Zamoyski, the cathedral (formerly a collegiate church) is the masterpiece of the Renaissance urban program. The vaults and facade were remodeled in Baroque style, but the original plan remains legible. Free access.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Eastern Poland: Primeval Forests, Painted Villages and Orthodox Heritage
From Zamość to Szydłów: Eastern Poland in 7 Days

visits.text: Since 1852, Zielona Góra transforms every September into Poland's wine capital. The Winobranie festival gathers more than 130 stands around Stary Rynek, a market square whose colorful facades reflect the bourgeois opulence of old Prussian Grünberg. Outside festival season, the square stays lively: terraces, central fountain, pastel-colored historic buildings. Avoid September weekends without a reservation.

The scent of must drifts through the Wine and Vine Museum, housed in a historic cellar in the city center. You'll understand why Zielona Góra claims to be Poland's northernmost wine-growing city: vines were already cultivated here before the Reformation. The collection traces local viticulture cycles, interrupted in 1945 and relaunched since the 1990s. Modest admission, allow 45 minutes.

The Palmiarnia, an unexpected tropical greenhouse in the heart of a Polish provincial town. Orchids, palm trees, giant cacti in a late 19th-century building - you enter out of curiosity and stay for the gentle humid warmth. Closed Mondays. Allow 30 minutes. For city views, climb the Wieża Ciśnień (water tower converted into a viewing platform) 15 minutes away on foot.

activities.text: If you visit in September, hop on a "winobus": special shuttles take visitors to Lubusz vineyards for tastings at producers' estates. Outside the festival, the Muzeum Lubuskie organizes occasional tastings. The Lubuskie Wine and Honey Trail offers a marked circuit of downtown cellars, walkable in 2 hours.

Have a glass of local Riesling in a historic downtown cellar - Piwnica Augusta Gremplera (Palmiarnia) or Piwnica Gustava Seelera (Plac Matejki) are the most accessible. Lubusz wines are crisp and low in alcohol, perfect before dinner. Count on 20 to 30 PLN for a glass with a local charcuterie board.

days_plan.itin_title: 8 Days in Lubusz - Fortresses, Lakes and the Oder Valley

visits.text: Water plunges 10 meters through the darkness of the Black Gallery at the Złoty Stok Gold Mine—Poland's only underground waterfall. The spectacle is amplified by the acoustics of the marble mining chamber. The gallery is crossed on a walkway at the edge of the precipice. Bring a light rain jacket: spray reaches curious visitors who venture too close.

Over seven centuries of operation, the Złoty Stok mine yielded roughly 16 tonnes of pure gold. In the 16th century, production reached 150 kg per year—about 8% of European output. The mine closed in 1962, was drained, then reopened as a tourist route in 1996. The 500-meter Gertrude Gallery traces medieval mining techniques with period models and tools.

activities.text: Ride the orange underground tram for the 300-meter return section—the most improbable option on the entire route. At the end of the tour, try gold panning in the stream: a few flakes guaranteed for kids. Total tour duration 1.5 hours. Online booking advised in July–August.

days_plan.itin_title: Kotlina Kłodzka in 10 Days: A Loop Through Waters and Stone

visits.text: Built between 1741 and 1747 as the Zamoyski family's private chapel, the kościół na wodzie at Zwierzyniec floats on an islet in the middle of a pond. The Baroque interior is tiny. Free parking by the basin, donation at the entrance.

Man-made, but the Stawy Echo still make an impression: forest ponds created in the 1930s on the Świerszcz stream, ringed by pines and dunes. Swimming is allowed in summer. Expect 6 PLN entry to the National Park to access them.

Push on to Browar Zwierzyniec, a historic brewery still in operation. The brick building dates from the nineteenth century. You can drink a local beer for a few zlotys in a surprisingly industrial setting deep in the forest.

activities.text: If you arrive early, the red trail to Stawy Echo crosses a pine forest in thirty minutes from the education center. The route is flat and pushchair-friendly (except the final stairs). Alternative: blue trail via the dune.

The Park's Ośrodek Edukacyjno-Muzealny (education and museum center) is worth a stop: exhibition on Roztocze wildlife, models, and an information point for all trails. Entry included in the park ticket.
